Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd spend maybe 20 hours a week on coursework, but it wasn't like a structured thing. I'm more of a hands-on learner, so I'd often be doing things away from the screen - sketching out concepts, building models, that kind of stuff. The actual sitting-down-and-studying part happened whenever I felt like it, which wasn't always the smartest approach.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, man. Some weeks I'd be super into it, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until something was due. I didn't have a set schedule - I just kind of went with the flow. Deadlines would sometimes wake me up, but honestly, I missed a lot of them anyway. Life stuff got in the way more than I'd like to admit.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I got a notification about something new, or when I remembered I should probably check what was happening in the course. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch the videos because those were actually pretty engaging. The gamified tasks they introduced were cool too - those badges and points made me want to check in more often. But it wasn't always about actually doing work, you know?
Q4: Yeah, for a while there I was logging in pretty regularly, like eight times a week or so. The gamified stuff they added made it feel less like a chore. I'd check my progress, see if I earned any new badges, watch some videos. It became kind of like checking social media - just something I'd do. But that didn't always translate into actually completing assignments.
Q5: The videos were my jam - I watched a ton of them, like over 86 minutes worth. I learn better when I can see and hear things being explained rather than just reading. The discussion boards were decent too because I could bounce ideas off other people. The gamified tasks were actually pretty motivating - they made the whole thing feel less boring and more like a game I wanted to win.
Q6: The reading materials and written assignments? Yeah, I pretty much ignored those. Reading walls of text just doesn't work for my brain - I need to be doing something, moving, engaging physically with the material. I'd skim stuff and think I'd come back to it later, but later never really came. The assignments felt overwhelming so I kept putting them off until it was too late.
Q7: If I could actually do something with my hands or see immediate results, that felt worth my time. The gamified tasks were great because I got instant feedback - points, badges, progress bars filling up. Videos where someone demonstrated concepts were useful. Anything that felt like busy work or just reading for the sake of reading? That felt like a waste of time to me.
Q8: Required stuff felt like pressure, which made me want to avoid it even more. Optional activities, especially the gamified ones, felt like I was choosing to engage, which made them more appealing. It's weird because I'd spend time on optional stuff that didn't count toward my grade while ignoring the assignments that actually mattered. The lack of pressure made optional things more fun.
Q9: Oh definitely. I'd download videos and watch them later when I was commuting or just chilling. I'd take notes by hand because writing helps me remember better than typing. Sometimes I'd look up YouTube videos or other explanations because they'd show things differently. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, just thinking through problems while doing other stuff.
Q10: There were tons of times I was learning but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd watch a downloaded video multiple times, sketch out diagrams in my notebook, or talk through concepts with my roommate. Sometimes I'd be working on understanding something while I was at my job or on the bus. The LMS only saw when I clicked things, not when I was actually processing information in my head.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then use it repeatedly offline. I don't always have great internet access at home since money's tight, so downloading stuff and studying offline was necessary. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have reviewed it five or six times on my own. That's just how I had to work with limited resources.
Q12: Mid-semester got rough. I was working more hours because I needed the money, and the coursework started piling up. Once I fell behind on a few assignments, it felt impossible to catch up. I kept logging in and watching videos, participating in discussions, but the actual assignment completion just tanked. It wasn't that I didn't care - I was just overwhelmed and didn't know where to start.
Q13: Yeah, definitely. When I was tired from work, I'd log in but just kind of browse around without really engaging deeply. Confusion about what was expected made me avoid certain tasks. When I had competing responsibilities like rent being due or work shifts, school took a backseat. I'd still check the LMS because it was easy, but doing the hard work of assignments? That required energy I didn't always have.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd scroll through materials, click on things, maybe start watching a video but zone out halfway through. My mind would be elsewhere - thinking about bills, work drama, whatever. I was physically present on the LMS but mentally checked out. It probably looked like engagement from the data perspective, but I wasn't really absorbing anything meaningful.
Q15: The assignments were my weakness. I knew they were important - my assignment scores were actually really good when I did them - but I kept putting them off. The bigger they seemed, the more I avoided them. It's like this mental block where starting felt harder than just not doing it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow became never. Classic procrastination spiral.
Q16: Deadlines would create these panic moments where I'd suddenly become super active. But by then it was often too late to do quality work, or I'd just miss them entirely. The gamified tasks had shorter, more frequent deadlines which worked better for me. Big, far-off deadlines? Those were basically invisible to me until they were right on top of me, and by then the damage was done.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback from the gamified stuff, it made me want to engage more. Those little dopamine hits from earning points or badges kept me coming back. But feedback on assignments I'd failed or missed? That just made me feel worse and want to avoid the course more. It's a cycle - good feedback pulled me in, bad feedback pushed me away.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I needed more structure than I gave myself. The gamified elements added some structure that helped. But overall, having 20 online courses to juggle meant I couldn't give any single one the attention it probably needed.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement way easier - they broke things into smaller chunks and made progress visible. The videos being available to download helped with my internet situation. What made it harder was the sheer volume of reading and written assignments. For a kinesthetic learner like me, more interactive elements would have been helpful. Less text, more doing.
Q20: That's literally me, man. I was on the LMS a lot, watching videos, checking discussions, doing the gamified stuff. But when it came to actually submitting assignments? I just couldn't get it together. Sometimes you're engaged with the content but not with the formal requirements. Or you're dealing with life stuff that makes the final step of submitting work feel impossible even when you understand the material.
Q21: I've seen people like that - they're efficient. They know exactly what they need, they get it, they study offline, they perform. My situation was kind of the opposite. I was highly active but inefficient. But yeah, low LMS activity doesn't mean someone isn't learning. They might just have a different approach that works better for them than constantly being online.
Q22: The log can show when you clicked, how long you were on a page, what you submitted. But it can't show if you actually understood something, if you were engaged mentally, or if you were learning offline. It misses the thinking, the processing, the real understanding. It's like measuring how long someone sits in a gym versus how fit they actually are - presence doesn't equal progress.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning, wanting to understand it, thinking about it even when you're not in class. It's not just showing up or clicking through modules. For me, I was engaged with the content - I found it interesting, I thought about it, I watched the videos multiple times. But I wasn't engaged with the formal requirements of the course. There's a difference.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually enjoyed the material and learned a lot, even though my grades don't reflect that. It wouldn't show my financial stress, my work schedule, my struggles with written assignments despite understanding concepts. It wouldn't show that I'm a hands-on learner stuck in a text-heavy system. The data tells a story, but it's missing most of the context that explains why things went the way they did.
